title: Testing libCellML
content: testing Testing libCellML Every pull request against the libCellML prime repository is tested as a matter of course, but there might be times when you’d like to test your own code without making a pull request. There are two ways you can do this. Using the Buildbot site Buildbot extlink-buildbot 0 https://buildbot.net/ ForceScheduler extlink-forcescheduler 0 http://docs.buildbot.net/latest/developer/cls-forcesched.html Buildbot extlink-buildbot 0 https://buildbot.net/ d.nickerson@auckland.ac.nz mailto:d.nickerson@auckland.ac.nz Buildbot extlink-buildbot 0 https://buildbot.net/ htpasswd extlink-htpasswd 0 https://httpd.apache.org/docs/current/programs/htpasswd.html has the capacity to build a libCellML compliant repository through the use of a. To make use of this facility, you will need to authenticate with thesystem. Access granted on request to David Nickerson. Thesystem uses MD5 encrypted passwords as created by. With your request to David include the output of this command: http://www.htaccesstools.com/htpasswd-generator/ http://www.htaccesstools.com/htpasswd-generator/ libCellML Buildbot extlink-libcellml_buildbot 0 https://autotest.bioeng.auckland.ac.nz/buildbot/libcellml/#/builders The websitecan be used if you do not have access to the htpasswd application. When your request has been dealt with, you will be able to login toand submit code for testing. using-the-buildbot-site using\ the\ buildbot\ site Manually on your local computer build/test code When your libCellML has built successfully, and with tests included, executables for the tests will be located in thedirectory where your library has been built. Unit tests make test code Run all of the unit tests using. tests/ code test_classname code classname code tests/test_classname code Individual C++ unit tests may be run by locating the test executable within the, directory. Unit tests for a given class are named, whereis the lowercase name of the class to test. These files are executable, so can be run from the command line. tests/bindings/python/ code python test_classname.py code classname code To run individual Python tests, navigate first into thedirectory. The test files can then be run individually as needed using, whereis the lowercase name of the class to test. - Coverage tests TODO make llvm_coverage code Coverage tests make sure that each line of code is covered by one or more unit tests. - A summary of coverage of the C++ code is accessible through, which result in statistics of line coverage. - For detailed information regarding specific lines in each file that need test coverage, use the command. Formatting tests TODO make test_clang_format code Check that the code is correctly formatted using the, if you have CLang installed.Check what other OS use? Memory tests TODO - manually-on-your-local-computer manually\ on\ your\ local\ computer testing-libcellml testing testing\ libcellml testing /home/runner/work/libcellml/libcellml/libcellml/docs/testing.rst {'total': 0, 'translated': 0} https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/

title: Development setup
content: setup Development setup working copy This section describes how someone wanting to contribute to the libCellML project should set up theirfor developing libCellML. Overview Github extlink-github 0 https://github.com/ Git extlink-git 0 https://git-scm.com/ The libCellML codebase is hosted onand thereforeis used to track changes. Before you begin, you will need to have a few pre-requisites satisfied: If Python bindings are required, the following packages are also needed: Github extlink-github 0 https://github.com/ andre user account (for the rest of this document we will call our user); Git extlink-git 0 https://git-scm.com/ for tracking changes in code; CMake extlink-cmake 0 https://cmake.org/ to configure the build files; Toolchain for building the library (dependent on the operating system); libxml2 extlink-libxml2 0 http://xmlsoft.org/ , an external library used to parse XML; zlib extlink-zlib 0 https://www.zlib.net/ , an external library used for compression; and Doxygen extlink-doxygen 0 http://www.doxygen.nl/ to generate documentation. arabic . shortlist Python extlink-python 0 https://www.python.org/ Version 3.5.2 or later is needed for Sphinx (see below) SWIG extlink-swig 0 http://swig.org to generate the bindings for Python Sphinx extlink-sphinx 0 http://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/master/ to generate documentation for Python bindings loweralpha . shortlist overview overview Pre-requisite acquisition In this section, we cover the retrieval and installation of pre-requisites. setup-git Git Github extlink-github 0 https://github.com/ Github sign-up page extlink-github 0 https://github.com//join Creating auser account is straightforward and can be done on the. Installing a Git client is particular to each operating system and some pointers are offered below: Windows Git for windows Git for windows http://gitforwindows.org/ GitHub Desktop GitHub Desktop https://desktop.github.com/ GitKracken GitKracken https://www.gitkraken.com/ Git SCM Git SCM https://git-scm.com/ Git is available from a variety of vendors. We commonly use, but other popular implementations are: --- Linux sudo apt-get install git code Git can be installed using the package manager with the command. macOS Git is pre-installed and available from the command line. - setup-cmake git setup-git git setup_git CMake CMake extlink-cmake 0 https://cmake.org/ is the cross-platform family of tools designed to build, test and package software. CMake is used to control the software compilation process using simple platform and compiler independent configuration files, and to generate native makefiles and workspaces that can be used in the compiler environment of your choice. Note that CMake version 3.2 or later is required to configure libCellML. Again, installation of CMake is particular to each operating system. Windows installation binaries extlink-cmake 0 https://cmake.org//download/ CMake provides. Choose the binary appropriate for your operating system and follow the installation instructions. Linux sudo apt-get install cmake code CMake can be installed using the package manager with the command. macOS installation binaries extlink-cmake 0 https://cmake.org//download/ CMake provides. Choose the binary appropriate for your operating system and follow the installation instructions. - setup-toolchain cmake setup-cmake cmake setup_cmake Toolchain The toolchain specifies the compiler that we will use to build libCellML. Toolchains are highly dependent on the operating system. When we test libCellML, we currently use: We recommend using these compilers on these systems, but feel free to use a different toolchain. The following sub-sections provide guidance on how to install the recommended toolchain on the major operating systems that libCellML supports. Windows Microsoft Visual Studio extlink-msvs 0 https://visualstudio.microsoft.com ; Linux GCC extlink-gcc 0 https://gcc.gnu.org/ ; and macOS Clang extlink-clang 0 https://clang.llvm.org/ . - Windows the MSVS download page extlink-msvs 0 https://visualstudio.microsoft.com/downloads/ Community Visual Studio is available to download from. We currently test with Visual Studio 2015 (version 14), but later versions are known to work. Theedition is more than sufficient for the needs of libCellML. To minimise the size of the installation, you may install only the C++ compiler. This component (and its requirements) is sufficient for building libCellML. Linux TODO macOS TODO - setup-zlib toolchain setup-toolchain toolchain setup_toolchain zlib zlib extlink-zlib 0 https://www.zlib.net/ is a common compression library. It is important to use a 64-bit version of the library as the 32-bit is not compatible with libCellML. Windows CMLibs zlib releases page extlink-cmlibs_zlib_repo 0 https://github.com/CMLibs-Dependencies/zlib/releases zlib extlink-zlib 0 https://www.zlib.net/ On Windows, it is easiest to install using the packaged version available from theon GitHub. You are welcome to build your own version if you would rather, but please make sure it is a 64-bit implementation. To buildfrom source refer to the next sub-section. Linux sudo apt install zlib1g-dev code For Debian-based systems, zlib can be installed using. macOS zlib is already installed on macOS, so no further action is required on that platform. - Building from source To create a zlib library suitable for libCellML, we need to download, configure, build, and install it. https://github.com/CMLibs-Dependencies/zlib https://github.com/CMLibs-Dependencies/zlib To download zlib, clone the repository: Create a build directory and change into it: Create the zlib library build instructions using CMake: <ZLIB_INSTALL_DIR> code Whereis to be replaced by a path on your computer. Something like: Andre code if your username was. We can now build the library with the command: The last thing we do is to install the library with the command: setup-libxml2 building\ from\ source building-from-source zlib setup-zlib zlib setup_zlib libxml2 libxml2 extlink-libxml2 0 http://xmlsoft.org/ is a parser and toolkit for manipulating XML files and text. It is important to use a 64-bit version of the library as the 32-bit is not compatible with libCellML. Windows CMLibs libxml2 releases page extlink-cmlibs_libxml2_repo 0 https://github.com/CMLibs-Dependencies/libxml2/releases libxml2 extlink-libxml2 0 http://xmlsoft.org/ On Windows, it is easiest to install using the packaged version available from theon GitHub. You are welcome to build your own version if you would rather, but please make sure it is a 64-bit implementation. To buildfrom source refer to the next sub-section. Linux sudo apt install libxml2-dev code For Debian-based systems, libxml2 can be installed using. macOS libxml2 is already installed on macOS, so no further action is required on that platform. - Building from source To create a libxml2 library suitable for libCellML, we need to download, configure, build, and install it. libxml2 can be configured to use zlib, so build and install that first before following these instructions. https://github.com/CMLibs-Dependencies/libxml2 https://github.com/CMLibs-Dependencies/libxml2 To download libxml2, clone the repository: Create a build directory and change into it: Create the libxml2 library build instructions using CMake: <ZLIB_INSTALL_DIR> code <LIBXML2_INSTALL_DIR> code <LIBXML2_INSTALL_DIR> code Whereis to be replaced by the path on your computer where zlib is installed.also needs to be replaced by a path on your computer. Using the same directory that the zlib library is installed to is a good idea. Following this advice we would setto: <ZLIB_INSTALL_DIR> code andwould be given the same value. We can now build the library with the command: The last thing we do is to install the library with the command: setup-doxygen building\ from\ source id1 libxml2 setup-libxml2 libxml2 setup_libxml2 Doxygen Doxygen extlink-doxygen 0 http://www.doxygen.nl/ is software which assembles documentation files directly from annotated source code, including the generation of inheritance and dependency tree diagrams for classes. In order for libCellML to build its documentation you will need to have Doxygen and its dependencies installed. Note that this does not affect your use of the libCellML library itself. Windows download page of the Doxygen website extlink-doxygen 0 http://www.doxygen.nl//download.html#srcbin download Graphviz extlink-graphviz 0 http://graphviz.org/download/ Binary distributions are available from the. You may also need to download and install the Graphviz package in order to generate the class hierarchy diagrams. You canfrom their website. Linux download page of the Doxygen website extlink-doxygen 0 http://www.doxygen.nl//download.html#srcbin Binary distributions are available from the. macOS download page of the Doxygen website extlink-doxygen 0 http://www.doxygen.nl//download.html#srcbin Binary distributions are available from the. - setup-python doxygen setup-doxygen doxygen setup_doxygen Python (optional) Python extlink-python 0 https://www.python.org/ Building LibCellML std std-ref 1 configure_and_build#configure-and-build is an open-source language which is syntactically easier to read than other higher-level languages. It is commonly used as a scripting language for experimentation, and is included here to allow libCellML to be accessed from Python-based programs. Note that creation of Python bindings is optional, and can be enabled/disabled at configuration time - there will be instructions for this on thepage. Windows the Python download page extlink-python 0 https://www.python.org//downloads/windows/ Python binaries for Windows can be found on, or you can build your own from source files if you prefer. Linux Python download page extlink-python 0 https://www.python.org//downloads/source/ Linux natively includes Python, but depending on the age of your machine and system installation, you may like to upgrade to a more recent version from the. macOS later version from the Python site extlink-python 0 https://www.python.org/downloads/mac-osx/ Python is already installed, though depending on the age of your machine you may like to upgrade it to a. - setup-swig python-optional setup-python python\ (optional) setup_python SWIG for Python bindings (optional) SWIG extlink-swig 0 http://swig.org Python extlink-python 0 https://www.python.org/ is a tool which acts as a translator between code written in C++ and other languages, including. Here SWIG is used to generate the optional Python bindings for libCellML. The current version (0.9) of libCellML uses SWIG 4.0.0. Windows Swigwin download page extlink-swigwin_download 0 https://sourceforge.net/projects/swig/files/swigwin/ SWIG for Windows is available from the. Linux Both Python and SWIG are already included, so no further action is needed. macOS downloads for Unix/Linux sources extlink-swig 0 http://swig.org/download.html SWIG for macOS can be accessed using theand building them locally according to the instructions provided on that page. - setup-sphinx swig-for-python-bindings-optional setup-swig swig\ for\ python\ bindings\ (optional) setup_swig Sphinx for Python documentation (optional) Sphinx extlink-sphinx 0 http://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/master/ Installation page extlink-sphinx 0 http://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/master//usage/installation.html import error extlink-github_rtd 0 https://github.com/rtfd/readthedocs.org/issues/3812 is an additional documentation tool used here to assemble documentation of the Python bindings. Instructions for all operating systems can be found on their. The current version (2.0.1) needs Python version 3.5.2 or later (note that Python subversions 3.5.0 and 3.5.1 will still raise andespite being reported fixed since version Sphinx 1.7.2). setup-codebase sphinx-for-python-documentation-optional setup-sphinx sphinx\ for\ python\ documentation\ (optional) setup_sphinx pre-requisite-acquisition pre-requisite\ acquisition Setting up the codebase GitHub Desktop extlink-github_desktop 0 https://desktop.github.com/ The remainder of this document assumes that the above pre-requisites have been met, and covers setup from the command line. title: Roadmap
content: libcellmlroadmap Roadmap id2 Updated: 09 March 2021. Each milestone may consist of several ‘releases’ and future requirements may impact the design and implementation of earlier releases of libCellML. Major changes in the API will be accepted up to the release of libCellML version 1.0.0. Contents Roadmap id2 roadmap High level objectives id3 high-level-objectives Environment id4 environment Requirements id5 requirements Milestone 1: Python bindings, validation, code generation, and documentation id6 milestone-1-python-bindings-validation-code-generation-and-documentation Milestone 2: units, JavaScript, and resets id7 milestone-2-units-javascript-and-resets Milestone 3: DAE models id8 milestone-3-dae-models Milestone 4: advanced capabilities id9 milestone-4-advanced-capabilities contents contents contents High level objectives id3 libCellML is focused on CellML 2.0 and beyond. The implementation of libCellML should be driven by the requirements for supporting CellML 2.0 and beyond. libCellML should be designed to support the CellML specification with the flexibility for extra restrictions, constraints, or additions coming from future proposals for changing the specification. * libCellML should be able to parse models in CellML 2.0 and newer versions of the specification. libCellML will only serialise models to the current version of the specification. * high-level-objectives high\ level\ objectives Environment id4 This section will specify the environment for the development of libCellML. GitHub GitHub https://github.com/ CellML organisation CellML organisation https://github.com/cellml github github https://github.com/ cellml-organisation cellml\ organisation https://github.com/cellml to host the primary libCellML source repository and issue tracker under the(current and former editorial board members). SWIG SWIG http://www.swig.org/ emscripten emscripten https://emscripten.org/ swig swig http://www.swig.org/ emscripten emscripten https://emscripten.org/ Development language: primary implementation in C++ withPython bindings andbased WebAssembly for JavaScript bindings. CMake CMake http://www.cmake.org/ cmake cmake http://www.cmake.org/ Build:for generating cross-platform build rules. Buildbot Buildbot http://buildbot.net/ buildbot buildbot http://buildbot.net/ Test: usingon our own servers to run continuous integration testing. gtest gtest https://github.com/google/googletest/ gtest gtest https://github.com/google/googletest/ Test: unit testing to use. reStructuredText reStructuredText http://docutils.sourceforge.net/docs/ref/rst/restructuredtext.html restructuredtext restructuredtext http://docutils.sourceforge.net/docs/ref/rst/restructuredtext.html Documentation: written in. Doxygen Doxygen http://www.doxygen.org/ doxygen doxygen http://www.doxygen.org/ Documentation: API and source code examples will be documented using C++-stylecomments. libcellml.org libcellml.org https://libcellml.org Website:. website source website source https://github.com/libcellml/website-src website-source website\ source https://github.com/libcellml/website-src Website: content pulled from libCellML documentation, tutorials, and. Deploy: use Github actions to build artifacts for deployment. * Requirements id5 libcellml.org libcellml.org https://libcellml.org/ Documentation: made available on. Documentation: is amenable for inclusion in external documentation efforts Development: Agile, test driven development where: Functionality is more important than API stability in early releases. Release early and often. * pull request pull request https://help.github.com/articles/using-pull-requests GitHub GitHub https://github.com/ pull-request pull\ request https://help.github.com/articles/using-pull-requests github id1 https://github.com/ Development: code review prior to acceptance into the primary repository using thefeature on. Development: objectives are added and broken down into incremental tasks. Development: a single task should be no more than two weeks. * We should avoid using non-standard system libraries unless there is a compelling reason. Once features are available, the API can be fine tuned in consultation with the CellML community. requirements requirements environment environment Milestone 1: Python bindings, validation, code generation, and documentation id6 Python bindings. Wrap the libCellML API using SWIG to be able to generate Python bindings for the library. Package the bindings so they can be easily installed across Windows, Linux, and macOS. arabic . Load a CellML 2.0 model and validate it. reset code Validate models against the rules defined in the current draft of the CellML 2.0 specification (currently an active document, outstanding issues regarding the newconstruct are likely to require updates to the validation implementation). arabic . Code generation. Focus on non-DAE models. Generate code for any simulatable non-DAE CellML model in PMR (i.e., translated from CellML 1.0/1.1). Guide the code generated for a given model (e.g., a variable to be controlled from an external data source, SED-ML defined changes, etc.). arabic . Documentation available. API. Tutorials/documented code examples. Integrating libCellML into common IDEs (Visual Studio, Qt Creator, and PyCharm). Provide documentation on the installation and use of the Python bindings across Windows, Linux, and macOS. arabic . arabic . milestone-1-python-bindings-validation-code-generation-and-documentation milestone\ 1:\ python\ bindings,\ validation,\ code\ generation,\ and\ documentation Milestone 2: units, JavaScript, and resets id7 Units. Checking units within mathematical expressions. Debugging assistance for model authors regarding units. arabic . JavaScript. Use Emscripten to create a JavaScript API for libCellML. Provide a suitable packaged version of the JavaScript API for integration in common JavaScript environments (e.g., Node, Browser). Document the installation and use of the JavaScript API. arabic . Resets. Extend libCellML implementation to fully support resets. arabic . arabic . milestone-2-units-javascript-and-resets milestone\ 2:\ units,\ javascript,\ and\ resets Milestone 3: DAE models id8 DAE models. Code generation support for models with DAEs. arabic . arabic . milestone-3-dae-models milestone\ 3:\ dae\ models Milestone 4: advanced capabilities id9 High-order model manipulation (recall the discussion with Andrew McCulloch at the 8th CellML workshop). Again, it is outside the scope of libCellML, but helping tool developers provide these kinds of services is very important. arabic . arabic . milestone-4-advanced-capabilities milestone\ 4:\ advanced\ capabilities roadmap libcellmlroadmap roadmap libcellmlroadmap /home/runner/work/libcellml/libcellml/libcellml/docs/roadmap.rst {'total': 0, 'translated': 0} https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/

title: Review process
content: review-process Review process This chapter details the responsibilities of the repository owners, and will help contributors to understand how their code contributions will be assessed and reviewed. Check for the green tick libCellML Buildbot extlink-libcellml_buildbot 0 https://autotest.bioeng.auckland.ac.nz/buildbot/libcellml/#/builders Before accepting a tranche of work into the libCellML prime repository check that thehas tested and passed the code. The status of the code is shown in the last commit of a pushed group of commits in the pull request. The last commit will have a red cross for a failed build or a green tick for a passed build. Obviously, make sure that the last commit has a green tick before merging. The “Details” link at the bottom of the pull request web page will take you to the build records of the library. This page shows the results of the unit tests for each target operating system, the results of the coverage test, the results of the memory check test, and the results of the documentation build. gcovr extlink-gcovr 0 https://gcovr.com/ Coverage Statistics std std-ref 1 coverage_statistics#coverage-statistics The coverage statistics for the library (generated from) can be reached through thepage. check-for-the-green-tick check\ for\ the\ green\ tick Read the documentation TODO Update needed now that documentation is integrated into libcellml.org. The documentation for the project is built as part of the testing process. The Documentation Builder link (entry 2 in step 5) will take you to the build for the documentation. On this page, you can see the steps taken to build the documentation. In the last step of the build (step 7), there is a link ‘dox’ (entry 2) that will take you to the built documentation. Doxygen extlink-doxygen 0 http://www.doxygen.nl/ API Documentation extlink-api 0 /documentation/latest/api/classlibcellml_1_1 The documentation should be reviewed in its final format particularly those parts of the documentation that (should) have changed due to the current pull request. The API documentation (generated by) can be reached from thepages. Note that it may be some time before changes to the documentation are seen on that site, as there is currently not a live connection between the repository and the API documentation on this website. read-the-documentation read\ the\ documentation Resolving comments All comments on the pull request and associated issue should be responded to and satisfied. It’s useful if contributors include the Git commit number (a six or seven digit hexadecimal code) in which any requested code changes are made in their reply to reviewer comments. It is the reviewers’ responsibility to check that appropriate changes have been made before merging the pull request. resolving-comments resolving\ comments Coding standards Google C++ Style Guide extlink-google_style_guide 0 https://google.github.io/styleguide/cppguide.html Coding Standards std std-ref 1 coding_standards#coding-standards Currently, there is no fully-defined libCellML coding standards set, but thecan be considered a baseline for the standard of code that is expected for libCellML. See thedocument for deviations from this guideline. coding-standards coding\ standards Merging a pull request release_staging_<version number> Caution: before merging a pull request make sure that a release is not currently in progress. If a release is in progress, the prime repository will have a branch called(where <version number> is a semantic version number). When merging a pull request, the reviewer should add a comment so that the corresponding issue is closed. This can be done by adding a directive to the merge commit, like so: the Github article for a list of all directives extlink-github_help 0 https://help.github.com/articles/closing-issues-via-commit-messages/ where the numeral corresponds to the issue that needs to be closed. You can use other closing directives that will achieve the same outcome, please seethat will work on Github. merging-a-pull-request merging\ a\ pull\ request review-process id1 review\ process review_process /home/runner/work/libcellml/libcellml/libcellml/docs/review_process.rst {'total': 0, 'translated': 0} https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/

title: Release process for
content: release-process libCellML Release process for libCellML The purpose of this document is to describe the process for preparing, reviewing, creating, and finalising releases ofusing GitHub Actions Continuous Integration (CI). libCellML cellml/libcellml cellml/libcellml https://github.com/cellml/libcellml cellml-libcellml cellml/libcellml https://github.com/cellml/libcellml The target audience of this document are the developers ofwho have write authority to therepository. Releases are made using GitHub Actions CI and consist of four steps: Each step must be performed in order. Later steps assume that all previous steps have completed successfully. Step 1 - Preparing the release Step 1 - Preparing the release step-1-preparing-the-release Step 2 - Checking the release Step 2 - Checking the release step-2-checking-the-release Step 3 - Make the release Step 3 - Make the release step-3-make-the-release Step 4 - Finalising the release Step 4 - Finalising the release step-4-finalising-the-release arabic . Prerequisites For all steps in the release process, you must: cellml/libcellml Be a maintainer (have write access) of theGitHub repository. Be familiar with semantic versioning. Ensure that no other release is currently in progress. main Choose thebranch as the branch to run the workflow from. - GitHub Actions run workflow branch chooser. {'*': 'images/release_process/workflow_branch_chooser.png'} ./images/release_process/workflow_branch_chooser.png images/release_process/workflow_branch_chooser.png 25% Fig. 11 Run workflow from branch chooser interface on GitHub. center libcellml-release-process-workflow-branch-chooser libcellml_release_process_workflow_branch_chooser release-staging-v<version number> Merging pull requests while a release is under way is not recommended and has not been tested. Before starting a new release, check the repository for the presence of a branch named. The presence of such a branch indicates that a release is already in progress. prerequisites prerequisites Branch model overview main release libCellML release-staging-v<version number> Thebranch is the primary development branch where normal feature development occurs. Thebranch represents the base for all released versions of. Release staging branches, named, are temporary branches used to assemble, review, and validate a candidate release before it is finalised. branch-model-overview branch\ model\ overview Step 1 - Preparing the release Prepare Release TheGitHub Actions workflow creates a staged release candidate based on a specified source branch and version number. This workflow assembles all changes intended for the release into a dedicated staging branch and prepares them for review before validation and publication. Actions cellml/libcellml cellml/libcellml https://github.com/cellml/libcellml/actions/workflows/release-prepare.yml The workflow is manually triggered from thetab of therepository. GitHub Actions prepare release workflow interface. {'*': 'images/release_process/prepare_release_interface.png'} ./images/release_process/prepare_release_interface.png images/release_process/prepare_release_interface.png Fig. 12 Prepare Release interface on GitHub. center libcellml-release-process-prepare-release-interface libcellml_release_process_prepare_release_interface Procedure Prepare Release cellml/libcellml cellml/libcellml https://github.com/cellml/libcellml cellml/libcellml id1 https://github.com/cellml/libcellml Navigate to theworkflow in the GitHub Actions tab of therepository. Enter the version number for the release. main (Optional) Specify a source branch if different from the defaultbranch. Manually trigger the workflow. Monitor the workflow run for completion and check for any failures. release Review the pull request created from the staging branch into thebranch. arabic . procedure procedure Tasks performed The workflow performs the following tasks: Collects all changes for the release from the specified source branch. Generates a changelog summarising the pull requests included in the release. Updates version numbers throughout the codebase. Organises these changes into multiple commits to simplify review. release-staging-v<version number> Creates a release staging branch named. release Opens a pull request from the staging branch to thebranch. - tasks\ performed tasks-performed Versioning Semantic Versioning Semantic Versioning https://semver.org/ semantic-versioning semantic\ versioning https://semver.org/ Version numbers must follow semantic versioning rules. Seefor further information. Examples of valid version numbers include: The version number consists of: An official release is created without a suffix. Developer releases include a suffix and differ in that artifacts are not published to public registries or GitHub releases. There are no strict validation checks beyond parsing; however, invalid semantic version strings will cause the workflow to fail. 1.0.0 1.0.0-alpha 1.0.0-alpha.1 1.0.0-beta 1.0.0-beta.2 1.0.0-rc.1 1.0.0-rc.1+build.1 1.0.0-post.1 - A core version (major, minor, patch). An optional suffix indicating a development or pre-release. - versioning versioning Results On successful completion: release-staging-v<version number> A staging branch () is created. release A pull request targeting thebranch is opened for review. - results results Failure handling If the workflow fails: Recommended actions: The pull request will not be created. The staging branch may still exist depending on when the failure occurred. - Review the workflow logs to identify the failure. Delete or reuse the staging branch as appropriate. Apply fixes to the source branch. Re-run the workflow once the issue has been resolved. - failure\ handling failure-handling Next step Step 2 - Checking the release Step 2 - Checking the release step-2-checking-the-release Once the staging pull request has been created and is ready for validation, proceed to. next\ step next-step step-1-preparing-the-release step\ 1\ -\ preparing\ the\ release Step 2 - Checking the release Check Release Step 1 - Preparing the release Step 1 - Preparing the release step-1-preparing-the-release TheGitHub Actions workflow validates the staged release candidate created in. It ensures that the code builds correctly and that automated tests have been executed before proceeding with the release. Procedure Check Release cellml/libcellml cellml/libcellml https://github.com/cellml/libcellml cellml/libcellml id3 https://github.com/cellml/libcellml Navigate to theworkflow in the GitHub Actions tab of therepository. Optionally adjust the configuration parameters for the end-to-end tests using the GitHub Actions interface. Manually trigger the workflow. Review the results published as comments on the staging pull request. arabic . GitHub Action check release manual trigger interface. {'*': 'images/release_process/check_release_interface.png'} ./images/release_process/check_release_interface.png images/release_process/check_release_interface.png 25% Fig. 13 Check Release workflow interface from GitHub Action. center libcellml-release-process-check-release-interface libcellml_release_process_check_release_interface procedure id2 Tasks performed The workflow performs the following tasks on the staged release code: Builds the codebase. Runs all unit tests. Reports on status of unit tests. Runs end-to-end tests. Reports on status of end-to-end tests along with the metadata used for running the tests. - tasks\ performed id4 Results Results from the workflow are published as comments on the staging pull request: These comments must be reviewed before proceeding to the next step. Unit test results : A summary indicating whether all tests passed. End-to-end test results : A summary of the outcome along with the metadata used during test execution. - GitHub Action check release unit test passing comment. {'*': 'images/release_process/check_release_unit_test_comment.png'} ./images/release_process/check_release_unit_test_comment.png images/release_process/check_release_unit_test_comment.png 67% Fig. 14 Check Release Builder Example of the comment left by theshowing the unit tests have passed. center libcellml-release-process-check-release-unit-test-comment libcellml_release_process_check_release_unit_test_comment GitHub Action check release end-to-end passing comment. {'*': 'images/release_process/check_release_end_to_end_comment.png'} ./images/release_process/check_release_end_to_end_comment.png images/release_process/check_release_end_to_end_comment.png 67% Fig. 15 Check Release Builder Example of the comment left by theshowing the unit tests have passed. center libcellml-release-process-check-release-end-to-end-comment libcellml_release_process_check_release_end_to_end_comment results id5 Success criteria A release candidate is considered valid when: If end-to-end tests fail: required ✅ All unit tests pass (). ⚠️ End-to-end tests may fail, but this has consequences. - may still proceed The release, however: not The user documentation willbe updated as part of the release process. The metadata recorded in the workflow comment will be used by subsequent workflows to finalise documentation updates on the libcellml.org website. - - success-criteria success\ criteria Failure handling unit tests fail If: must not proceed The release. The staged release should be removed and fixes applied to the source branch before starting the release process again. - end-to-end tests fail If, either: Continue with the release, understanding that the user documentation will not be updated. Or, fix the issues stemming from the end-to-end testing and re-run the workflow. - - failure\ handling id6 Next step Step 3 - Make the release Step 3 - Make the release step-3-make-the-release Once all required checks have passed and the results have been reviewed, the release process can continue with. next\ step id7 step-2-checking-the-release step\ 2\ -\ checking\ the\ release Step 3 - Make the release Make Release TheGitHub Actions workflow creates a release on GitHub and builds all the release assets. This workflow behaves differently depending on the type of release: Yggdrasil Yggdrasil https://github.com/JuliaPackaging/Yggdrasil yggdrasil yggdrasil https://github.com/JuliaPackaging/Yggdrasil libCellML In addition, the workflow creates a pull request to the Julia packaging repository,, to updating thepackage. The pull request to Yggdrasil must be manually managed. Historically, the Julia package maintainers have been very good at approving and merging pull requests in a timely manner. Make Release YGGDRASIL_PR_PAT cellml/Yggdrasil cellml/Yggdrasil https://github.com/cellml/yggdrasil cellml/libcellml cellml/libcellml https://github.com/cellml/libcellml cellml/libcellml cellml/libcellml https://github.com/cellml/libcellml cellml-yggdrasil cellml/yggdrasil https://github.com/cellml/yggdrasil cellml/libcellml id8 https://github.com/cellml/libcellml cellml/libcellml id9 https://github.com/cellml/libcellml Theworkflow makes use of a classic personal access token with write access and pull request access to therepository so that it can create a pull request to update the Julia package. This classic personal access token is stored as a secret () in therepository and is used by the workflow to create the pull request to Yggdrasil. It needs to be updated with a new token if the old token is revoked or expires. The current expiry date for the current token is 2026-06-23. Any maintainer of therepository can create a new token and update the secret if necessary. GitHub Action make release manual trigger interface. {'*': 'images/release_process/make_release_interface.png'} ./images/release_process/make_release_interface.png images/release_process/make_release_interface.png 25% Fig. 16 Make Release workflow interface from GitHub Action. center libcellml-release-process-make-release libcellml_release_process_make_release For official releases, it publishes binaries to GitHub and external registries. For developer (pre-)releases, it produces build artifacts only. - Procedure Make Release cellml/libcellml cellml/libcellml https://github.com/cellml/libcellml cellml/libcellml id11 https://github.com/cellml/libcellml Navigate to theworkflow in the GitHub Actions tab of therepository. Manually trigger the workflow (this workflow may take up to 20 minutes to run). Check the workflow run for any failures. Yggdrasil Yggdrasil https://github.com/JuliaPackaging/Yggdrasil yggdrasil id12 https://github.com/JuliaPackaging/Yggdrasil Monitor the pull request created on therepository. arabic . procedure id10 Tasks performed The workflow performs the following tasks Moves the staged code to the release branch. Creates a release on GitHub using information from the staging branch. Builds release assets including: Binaries Python wheels Javascript package Julia packaging update (pull request), and source code - Publishes release assets to GitHub and public registries (for official releases only). - tasks\ performed id13 Failure handling If there is a failure in the workflow that release will be incomplete, but should not be considered a failed release. The only solution is to fix the problems through the normal channels and start the release process again. failure\ handling id14 Next step Step 4 - Finalising the release Step 4 - Finalising the release step-4-finalising-the-release Once the release has been made and the assets have been published, the release process can continue with. next\ step id15 step-3-make-the-release step\ 3\ -\ make\ the\ release Step 4 - Finalising the release Finalise Release TheGitHub Actions workflow completes the release process by updating the libcellml.org website, integrating release changes back into the main development branch, and cleaning up release staging artefacts. This workflow is the final step in the release process and performs post-release housekeeping tasks. The API and developer documentation will be updated and any user documentation that passed the end-to-end testing will be updated on the website. If the release is considered a successful release: Regardless of the release outcome: GitHub Action finalise release manual trigger interface. {'*': 'images/release_process/finalise_release_interface.png'} ./images/release_process/finalise_release_interface.png images/release_process/finalise_release_interface.png 25% Fig. 17 Finalise Release workflow interface from GitHub Action. center libcellml-release-process-finalise-release libcellml_release_process_finalise_release The libcellml.org website will be updated with the latest applicable documentation. The release staging branch will be merged back into the main development branch. - The release staging branch will be deleted. The associated pull request will be closed. - Procedure cellml/libcellml cellml/libcellml https://github.com/cellml/libcellml cellml/libcellml id17 https://github.com/cellml/libcellml Navigate to the Finalise Release workflow in the GitHub Actions tab of therepository. Manually trigger the workflow. If the release was successful, enable the option to update the website and merge changes back into the main development branch. - Monitor the workflow run for completion and check for any failures. libcellml.org website libcellml.org website https://github.com/libcellml/website-src libcellml-org-website libcellml.org\ website https://github.com/libcellml/website-src Review the pull request created against the production branch of therepository, and merge it if the updates are correct. arabic . procedure id16 Tasks performed The workflow performs the following tasks: Generates API and developer documentation for the release and creates a pull request to update the website. Generates user documentation for components that passed end-to-end testing and updates the existing website update pull request. Updates the project version by appending a .post suffix. Merges the release staging branch back into the main development branch. Deletes the release staging branch and closes the associated draft pull request. - tasks\ performed id18 Failure handling If a failure occurs during this workflow, manual intervention may be required to resolve any issues. Recommended actions: Review the workflow logs to identify the source of the failure. Fix any issues in the staging or documentation repositories. Re-run the workflow once the problem has been resolved. - failure\ handling id19 step-4-finalising-the-release step\ 4\ -\ finalising\ the\ release release-process-for-libcellml release-process release\ process\ for\ libcellml release_process https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ /home/runner/work/libcellml/libcellml/libcellml/docs/release_process.rst {'total': 0, 'translated': 0}

title: Utilities
content: Utilities LIBCELLML_ENABLE_UTILITIES code TRUE code This document covers the utilities that are available to developers of libCellML. The utilities are not built or made available by default. The utilities can be enabled through the configuration of the library. In order to enable the utilities, set the configuration variableto. The available utilities are: Create MathML DTD files Update generator profile SHA-1 values arabic . Create MathML DTD files mathmldtd.h code mathmldtd.cpp code The create MathML DTD files utility generates a compressed MathML DTD. The MathML DTD is used to determine the validity of MathML strings in a CellML document. This utility updates the header fileand source filein the source tree of the current build. For a make-based environment, the utility may be run with the following command: create-mathml-dtd-files create\ mathml\ dtd\ files Update generator profile SHA-1 values generatorprofilesha1values.h code The update generator profile SHA-1 values utility calculates the SHA-1 values for the current C and Python generator profiles. This utility updates the header filein the source tree of the current build. title: Buildingfor use in the Browser
content: libCellML Buildingfor use in the Browser libCellML To buildfor the browser, we use the emscripten toolchain to create web assembly binaries. Here, we will go through the process of building web assembly binaries on macOS. For other operating systems, modifications to these instructions will have to be made. Any modifications required are expected to be self evident – with this in mind these instructions should be universally applicable. Preparation libCellML To buildfor the browser, we will need the following tools ready for use. Emscripten Compiler toolchain CMake (at least version 3.14) Git - preparation preparation Building libCellML libCellML libCellML requires two dependencies to be built beforeitself can be built. We will clone and build those first and then move on to building. Dependencies Start by creating a directory under which all our files will live and make it our current directory for subsequent commands: Now, we can clone our dependencies (we suggest using these exact repositories other repositories may work but these repositories are known to work): We will create two directories to hold the build files: zlib libxml2 zlib not Then, we will configure, build, and installas this is required by. We will installunder our emscripten working directory andinto the system directories: emcmake code zlib Release current source build Here, we have configured the project using the emscripten wrapper script. This script sets up environment variables for us to make building with the emscripten compilers easy. We also configure thelibrary to be built inmode and set the install prefix relative to thedirectory. However, this is not an issue for us as theanddirectories are sibling directories. zlib To build and install thelibrary, we simply issue these commands: libxml2 Next, we follow a similar procedure for building. Start by making the current directory the parent of the zlib build directory: Then, as before, configure the library: There are seven additions to the configure command this time: ZLIB_DIR code libxml2 As a general note, it is better to specify the variableas an absolute path and not a relative path as we have done here. Feel free to use the absolute path when you are configuring. Then, as before, we simply issue the build and install commands: libCellML That is all that is required for preparing the dependencies ready for. ZLIB_DIR code -DZLIB_DIR=../usr/local/lib/cmake/ZLIB-1.2.12/ code zlib We setto enable the configuration to find ourlibrary we just installed,. libxml2 We turn off building a sharedlibrary, -DBUILD_SHARED_LIBS=OFF. libxml2 We turn off buildingwith iconv, -DLIBXML2_WITH_ICONV=OFF. libxml2 We turn off buildingwith lzma compression, -DLIBXML2_WITH_LZMA=OFF. libxml2 We turn off buildingwith Python, -DLIBXML2_WITH_PYTHON. libxml2 We turn off buildingwith tests, -DLIBXML2_WITH_TESTS=OFF; and libxml2 We turn off buildingwith programs, -DLIBXML2_WITH_PROGRAMS=OFF. arabic . dependencies dependencies libCellML libCellML libCellML Following a similar approach to building the dependencies, we can now configure and build. We don’t installwhen building for web assembly, so there is no install command in the instructions. emscripten code To get started, we will make our current directory thedirectory we made at the start. Assuming we are in the directory where we left off from preparing the dependencies, we simply change to the parent directory: If your current directory is not where the dependency instructions left off then modify the command as appropriate for your situation. git clone code Now, let’s get the source code using: Now, we will create a build directory: We can now configure the library: LibXml2_DIR code As with the earlier note, it is best ifis an absolute path and not a relative path as shown here. The last thing we have to do is build the library: That should be everything done. libcellml libcellml building building Outcome libcellml.js code libcellml.wasm code libcellml.common.js code libcellml.common.wasm code src/bindings/javascript/ code libCellML Given that all of the above worked you should have the following four files,,, andin therelative to thebuild directory where we left off above. outcome outcome Test libCellML libCellML build-libcellml-release code We can test theWebAssembly to make sure everything works. From thebuild directory, we can run the test command: The first time the tests are run the node modules required for running the tests are installed. This can take a while to complete, please be patient while the node modules install. Subsequent running of the tests will not require node modules to be installed and the tests will be run in a much more timely manner. If all the tests passed, you should see: A few lines up from the bottom of the output from the above command. test test building-libcellml-for-use-in-the-browser building\ libcellml\ for\ use\ in\ the\ browser /home/runner/work/libcellml/libcellml/libcellml/docs/dev_building_web_assembly.rst {'total': 0, 'translated': 0} https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/

title: Current Thinking for libCellML
content: libcellmlcurrentthinking Current Thinking for libCellML This document simply outlines some of the current rationale that has an influence on how the codebase is developed. Temporarily dealing with external documents that are stored on the local file system (relative to the current CellML model document). Allows testing of the most common model import scenario (local files addressed with a relative URL). Absolves libcellml from fetching files and communicating across the Internet. Expect to provide another layer that would perform this role as a libCellML I/O library, which would allow the removal of this temporary inclusion. No avenue to retrieve remote external references. - Serialise and deserialise from a string. XML extlink-xml 0 https://www.w3.org/XML/ Present a useful interface not one tied to theserialisation structure. CellML extlink-cellml 0 https://www.cellml.org/ Validation is quite separate (you are free to make invalidmodels). Public API treats MathML as strings only. MathML extlink-mathml 0 https://www.w3.org/Math/ Internal to the code generation we are currently creating our ownobject model based on an abstract syntax tree. Minimal implementation to support the immediate requirement of code generation. Expect to provide another layer that would handle MathML as a separate thing, potentially linking back to the advanced functionality envisions for symbolic analysis of the model. Internal to the validator, the MathML strings are parsed into a DOM for use in schema validation against the MathML schema. - - current-thinking-for-libcellml libcellmlcurrentthinking current\ thinking\ for\ libcellml libcellmlcurrentthinking /home/runner/work/libcellml/libcellml/libcellml/docs/current_thinking.rst {'total': 0, 'translated': 0} https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/

title: Contributing to libCellML
content: contributing Contributing to libCellML prime repository xref std std-term 1 glossary#term-prime-repository GitHub extlink-github 0 https://github.com/ Git extlink-git 0 https://git-scm.com/ Setup std std-ref 1 setup#setup Building std std-ref 1 configure_and_build#configure-and-build This document covers the process to follow for getting your changes into the. While there are many types of contribution, this section is focussed on contributions made throughandto assets that are managed using the version control system. It is assumed thatandhave already been read and followed. Overview prime repository xref std std-term 1 glossary#term-prime-repository For any body of work intended for contribution to the, first start with a Github issue. The issue can be used to discuss the topic and clarify any problems related to it. Once progress has been made towards addressing the issue, a pull request is created that references the issue. Buildbot extlink-buildbot 0 https://buildbot.net/ build/test code Reviewers provide feedback on the changes by adding comments to the pull request or associated commits. Theprocedure will run each time changes are pushed to the pull request’s branch, and the results are displayed in the pull request view. prime repository xref std std-term 1 glossary#term-prime-repository develop code Once all the changes and reviews are complete, one of theowners will merge the pull request into the prime repository, onto thebranch. Note that a bug is just a type of issue, and that resolving the bug should have both the implementation to fix the bug and a test that triggers the bug. Fig. 8 std std-numref 1 libcellml-development-process Fig. %s gives a graphical overview of the developer contribution process. For more details, see the text below. Developer contribution process. {'*': 'images/libCellMLProcesses-DevelopmentProcess.png'} ./images/libCellMLProcesses-DevelopmentProcess.png images/libCellMLProcesses-DevelopmentProcess.png Fig. 8 Developer contribution process. center libcellml-development-process libcellml_development_process overview overview GitHub issues If an issue does not exist for the required work (e.g. implementation of a feature, fixing of a bug), then create a new one. The issue is the place to discuss the particulars related to the issue, discussions on determining the scope of the issue or clarification of any points that are unclear. https://github.com/cellml/libcellml/libCellML repository extlink-libcellml_repo 0 https://github.com/cellml/libcellml/libCellML repository Issues code To create a new issue, go to theGitHub page, and select thetab. Use the green button on the right hand side to create a new issue. You will need to add a description of what you’ve encountered, as well as any small snippets of code that help describe it. Labels TODO These are not current? A Github issue may be assigned labels by the project administrators to help identify its status at a glance. General labels currently used for libCellML are: Platform Milestone In addition, alabel may be used to identify the issue as specific to a given platform.labels may be used to project when a feature is expected to be complete and/or indicate the priority of a given issue. Higher priority issues will take precedence and therefore be assigned a more immediate (lower) milestone number. Bug : the issue identifies a malfunction in the current codebase. Feature : the issue constitutes a request or plan for a new feature. Needs tests : the issue requires test(s) to be complete. This may refer to a bug report, contributed code, comments, etc. in the issue. Needs documentation : the issue requires documentation to be complete. This may refer to a bug report, contributed code, comments, etc. in the issue. Needs reviewing : the issue requires further review from project participants to be complete. This may refer to a bug report, contributed code, comments, etc. in the issue. * labels labels github-issues github\ issues Topic branches prime repository xref std std-term 1 glossary#term-prime-repository prime repository xref std std-term 1 glossary#term-prime-repository develop code A topic or feature branch is a branch that is local to you (and anyone you collaborate with), it is a branch that will not be available from the. All development work should be carried out on a topic branch, for example any major feature that you work on or minor bug fix. Before creating a local topic branch, pull the latest changes from thebranch, to make sure that you’re working from the most up-to-date version of the code. prime repository xref std std-term 1 glossary#term-prime-repository develop code Following this process will make it easier to have multiple topic branches at once and keep them in-sync with thebranch, which will in turn make it easier to manage multiple pull requests. issue #123 The following Git commands show an example of how to create a topic branch for fixing a (hypothetical) bug described in: Git branching illustration. {'*': 'images/libCellMLProcesses-GitBranching.png'} ./images/libCellMLProcesses-GitBranching.png images/libCellMLProcesses-GitBranching.png Fig. 9 How branching in a Git repository works. center git-branching git_branching topic-branches topic\ branches Test-driven development Test-driven development entails writing a test (or suite of tests) that covers the intended functionality, and no more. The tests will require some skeleton implementation so that they can compile, but in the first instance they probably won’t pass. Writing the test(s) first shows the other developers the new behaviour that you intend to implement. By making a pull request containing only these tests, other developers can then comment and discuss until everyone is happy with the new behaviour. Following this contribution process allows for corrections before time is spent on the functional code. It may be necessary to refactor the current design to enable the easiest possible way to add the new feature. This is a good thing as the quality of the design improves and this makes it easier to work with in the future. Refactoring means improving the code without adding features, and the tests provide validation that the refactored code performs as well as before. For simple or obvious bugs which have fallen through the testing gaps, simply submitting a pull request with the repaired implementation and no extra tests is fine. test-driven-development test-driven\ development GitHub pull requests Fig. 9 std std-numref 1 git-branching Fig. %s prime repository xref std std-term 1 glossary#term-prime-repository develop code Addresses issue #123 code Once some changes have been made and local commits committed, push your changes to your Github libCellML repository (refer to). From there, create a pull request from your topic branch to thebranch. When creating the pull request, make sure to add in the comment(of course, replace the number 123 with the actual number of the issue you are addressing), or something to that effect. This will create a link between the issue and the pull request enabling other people to see that you are working on this issue and comment on your work. The following Git commands show an example of how to add all files, commit the changes, and push them to a Github repository for the first time: git add code git commit code git push code issue123 code issue123 code Theandcommands should be obvious, thecommand sets the local branchto be linked with the remote branchin the origin (the default shorthand for your libCellML repository on Github) repository. This branch will be created in the origin repository if it does not already exist. creating Github pull requests extlink-github_help 0 https://help.github.com/articles/creating-a-pull-request/ To create a pull request from one Github repository to another, follow the instructions for. github-pull-requests github\ pull\ requests Reviewing It may happen that submitted work is not reviewed immediately or the work is finished before any comments have been made. If this is the case add a comment to the pull request asking for the submission to be reviewed. An email will be sent out to the repository owners who will respond and review the submission, please remember that everyone is busy and it may not happen right away. reviewing reviewing Satisfying the feedback It is important to respond to all feedback appropriately, the review process will check to make sure that all comments have been dealt with. Feel free to respond to comments as appropriate, e.g. through code changes, posting a direct reply, etc. Where you need to change code in response to a comment, be sure to include the commit code (a 7 digit hex number) in your reply, so that others can see where and how the changes were made. satisfying-the-feedback satisfying\ the\ feedback Completing prime repository xref std std-term 1 glossary#term-prime-repository To complete the process, it is required to have two owners of thecomment on the pull request that they are satisfied that the work on the issue is complete and also that the feedback has been addressed, in essence that they are happy to merge the submission. For small submissions, it is sufficient for the second owner to show satisfaction by performing the merge. For larger submissions one of the owners will post a comment on the issue notifying subscribers that they intend to merge the pull request. If no further objections are raised, the pull request will be merged and closed. Review Process std std-ref 1 review_process#review-process A little reminder for the repository owners to check that thehas been followed before merging the pull request. completing completing contributing-to-libcellml contributing contributing\ to\ libcellml contributing https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ /home/runner/work/libcellml/libcellml/libcellml/docs/contributing.rst {'total': 0, 'translated': 0}

title: Configuring and building libCellML
content: configure-and-build Configuring and building libCellML id2 This document covers building libCellML from source. Development Setup std std-ref 1 setup#setup It is assumed that you already have the codebase downloaded and ready for building. If not, please see thepage for instructions. LIBCELLML_SRC code LICENSE code The variableshall be used to refer to the directory containing thefile for libCellML. LIBCELLML_SRC code LIBCELLML_SRC code build code libcellml-build code LIBCELLML_BUILD code It is best to build libCellML outside of the source tree. Instructions and information about installing CMake can be found in thesection. title: Coding standards
content: coding-standards Coding standards Google C++ Style Guide extlink-google_style_guide 0 https://google.github.io/styleguide/cppguide.html The coding standard for libCellML follows that specified in the, but it does not have to be followed to the letter; other people have not so positive opinions about the quality of the Google style guide. The coding standard in use for libCellML is in evidence in the code itself. New code should be consistent with what is already there. In essence, we seek code that looks good, is easy-to-read and has great documentation, and - most importantly - does what it promises to do. We do not want to spend time discussing the minutiae of the coding style. The following is a list of exceptions/deviations from the Google style guide that have been agreed upon for libCellML software development purposes. camelCase extlink-wikipedia 0 https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Camel_case class method names. Indent core code four spaces at a time (no tabs); for CMake files use two space indentation. * Doxygen comments code code std::string code @c code Code-words (e.g.,,) should be styled as typewriter text with a preceding. Doxygen comments should be sentence-style: beginning with capitalisation (except code-words) and ending with punctuation. However, they do not need to form grammatically correct sentences. * doxygen-comments doxygen\ comments Test naming Names should respect the camel case naming convention. Names should be explicit enough to identify the specific code features they cover. * test-naming test\ naming Variable naming The following rules for naming of variables should be followed. mMyClassMemberVariable code Class member variables:. myFunctionParameterVariable code Function parameter variables:. myLocalVariable code Local variables:. * variable-naming variable\ naming Weak pointers std::weak_ptr<T>::lock() code nullptr code The return value ofshould only be tested if it may be equal to. * weak-pointers weak\ pointers coding-standards id1 coding\ standards coding_standards /home/runner/work/libcellml/libcellml/libcellml/docs/coding_standards.rst {'total': 0, 'translated': 0} https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/ https://www.sphinx-doc.org/

title: libcellml
content: CellmlElementType The CellmlElementType enum class. This enum describes CellML elements. A CellML element identifies elements in the underlying CellML specification format. The enum also adds an UNDEFINED entry for not specifying an underlying CellML element. AnalyserPtr Type definition for shared analyser pointer. AnalyserEquationPtr Type definition for shared analyser equation pointer. AnalyserEquationAstPtr Type definition for shared analyser equation AST pointer. AnalyserExternalVariablePtr Type definition for shared analyser external variable pointer. AnalyserModelPtr Type definition for shared analyser model pointer. GeneratorVariableTrackerPtr Type definition for shared generator context pointer. GeneratorProfilePtr Type definition for shared generator variable pointer. ImporterPtr Type definition for shared importer pointer. IssuePtr Type definition for shared issue pointer. LoggerPtr Type definition for shared parser pointer. ParserPtr Type definition for shared parser pointer. PrinterPtr Type definition for shared printer pointer. StrictPtr Type definition for shared strict pointer. ValidatorPtr Type definition for shared validator pointer. ComponentPtr Type definition for shared component pointer. ComponentEntityPtr Type definition for shared component entity pointer. EntityPtr Type definition for shared entity pointer. ImportedEntityPtr Type definition for shared imported entity pointer. ImportSourcePtr Type definition for shared import source pointer. ModelPtr Type definition for shared model pointer. ParentedEntityPtr Type definition for shared parented entity pointer. ResetPtr Type definition for shared reset pointer. UnitsItemPtr Type definition for shared units item pointer. UnitsPtr Type definition for shared units pointer. VariablePtr Type definition for shared variable pointer. VariablePairPtr Type definition for shared variable pair pointer. cellmlElementTypeAsString std::string Convert a CellmlElementType enumeration value into itsform. std::string Convert a CellmlElementType enumeration value into itsform. value A CellmlElementType enumeration. param std::string A. return value version Get the version number. Returns the version of the library as a number. An unsigned int to represent the version. return versionString Get the version string. The version string is in the format x.y.z, where the "."s are literal, and x,y and z represent counting numbers, in which case x is the major version, y the minor version, and z the patch level. A string to represent the version. return

Constructor for derived classes,. pImpl pFunc Getter for private implementation pointer,. pFunc Const getter for private implementation pointer,. addComponent Add a child component to this component entity. Add the given component as a child component of this component entity. If the given component doesn't have a parent then this component entity will be set as its parent. If the given component does have a parent then the given component will have this component entity set as its parent and its old parent will no longer have the given component as a child. If the addition of the given component creates a cycle of components the given component will not be added to this component entity and it will not have this component entity set as its parent. During the analysis of a model, various tests are performed and their result cached. So, if you test two variables that were tested during the analysis then the cached result will be returned otherwise the two variables will be properly tested and their result cached. This works because analways refers to a static version of a. title: libcellml::AnalyserExternalVariable
content: AnalyserExternalVariable classlibcellml_1_1AnalyserExternalVariable compound Theclass. AnalyserExternalVariable classlibcellml_1_1AnalyserExternalVariable compound Analyser classlibcellml_1_1Analyser compound GeneratorProfile::externalVariableMethodTypeDefinitionString classlibcellml_1_1GeneratorProfile_1ad41ac2a0d47d82a7fe1da22b9252fc4e member GeneratorProfile::setExternalVariableMethodTypeDefinitionString classlibcellml_1_1GeneratorProfile_1ad0f95a069343e940af0692ac4d294027 member Theclass is for representing an external variable in the context of a CellML, i.e. a variable which is not set or computed by this model, but set externally. It is therefore the responsibility of the user to set an external variable (seeand). Also, say that the analysis of a model fails because the type of one or several of its variables could not be determined (i.e. the model is underconstrained). To add those variables as external variables will allow the analysis to succeed. This is because the analyser will have considered those variables as initialised. Once again, it is the responsibility of the user to ensure that it all makes sense. mPimpl Private member to implementation pointer,. ~AnalyserExternalVariable Destructor,. content: build-from-source Build and install from source Contents 0: Gather the prerequisites id1 gather-the-prerequisites 1: Create the libCellML library id2 create-the-libcellml-library 2: Build and install the library id3 build-and-install-the-library 3: Test the installation: C++ id4 test-the-installation-c 4: Test the installation: Python id5 test-the-installation-python contents local contents contents 0: Gather the prerequisites id1 libxml2 False http://www.xmlsoft.org/ this page False https://github.com/CMLibs-Dependencies/libxml2/releases If you’re working on a Windows or Linux machine you’ll need to haveinstalled. It can be downloaded from. This is present by default on MacOS operating systems. SWIG False http://www.swig.org/ If you’d like to build the Python bindings, you’ll need to have access to. If you will only be working in C++ then you don’t need this step. gather-the-prerequisites 0:\ gather\ the\ prerequisites 1: Create the libCellML library id2 1.a Create an empty folder and navigate into it. dothis 1.b libcellml code Clone the branch from the libCellML library github repository. This will create and populate a folder called. dothis 1.c libcellml code source code build code install code Rename the clonedfolder to be, and create sibling folders calledand: dothis Your folder structure is now: Your folder structure should now look like this: create-the-libcellml-library 1:\ create\ the\ libcellml\ library 2: Build and install the library id3 2.a MyLibCellMLDirectory/build code Navigate into thedirectory to make the library: dothis -S code -B code CMake uses command line flags as well as the arguments’ positions to determine what each of your inputs means. In the example above theflag indicates the source directory, and theflag indicates the build directory. By default, if these flags are not present, CMake will take the first unflagged item as the source directory (if there are none then it uses the current directory), and the second (if there is no second then it uses the current directory) as the build directory. Thus, if called from the build directory, then these are equivalent: infospec 2.b From the same location, install the library: dothis 2.c (optional) build code If you intend to use the Python bindings, you will need to install the Python version of the local library using pip3. Again, this expects that you are still in thedirectory. dothis 2.d (optional) build code Run the tests on the library from thedirectory. title: A model of a sodium channel
content: sodium-channel A model of a sodium channel HH Tutorial 3 False https://libcellml.org/documentation/latest/tutorials/hh_tutorial3/index The second ion channel in Hodgkin-Huxley model controls sodium. The theory here is the basis for the the practical modelling tutorial in. Theory id3 theory Interpretation as a CellML model id4 interpretation-as-a-cellml-model Simulation and results id5 simulation-and-results Notable results id6 notable-results Next steps id7 next-steps contents local contents contents Theory id3 m m = 0 h h = 1 The Hodgkin-Huxley model’s sodium channel has two types of gate, an-gate (of which there are 3) that is initially closed () before activating and inactivating back to the closed state, and an-gate that is initially open () before activating and inactivating back to the open state. The short period when both types of gate are open allows a brief window current to pass through the channel. These were created using a timestep of 0.01ms to an ending time of 40ms using the. n n_{\infty} = \frac{\alpha_{n}}{\alpha_{n} + \beta_{n}} = n_{\infty} = \ At 0mV, the steady state value of the-gate is0.324 and, at -85mV,0.945. Figure 9 std std-numref True potassium-voltage Figure %s Figure 10 std std-numref True potassium-n-gate-response Figure %s Figure 11 std std-numref True potassium-conductance Figure %s Figure 12 std std-numref True potassium-current Figure %s n n = n = \alpha_{n} \beta_{n} = n^{4}\bar{g}_K n^{4} i_{K} = g_{Na}\left( V - E_{K} \right) The voltage traces are shown in. The-gate response inshows it opening beyond its initial partially open value of0.324 at 0mV, to plateau at an almost fully open state of0.945 at the Nernst potential of -85mV, before closing again as the voltage is stepped back to 0mV. Note that the opening behaviour (set by the voltage dependence of theopening rate constant) is faster than the closing behaviour (set by the voltage dependence of theclosing rate constant). The channel conductance () is shown in. Note the initial s-shaped conductance increase caused by the effect of the four gates in serieseffect on conductance. Finally the channel currentis shown in. There is no current flow during the time when the voltage is clamped at the Nernst potential (-85mV) when the gate is opening. When the voltage is stepped back to 0mV the open gates begin to close and the conductance declines, but as there is a voltage gradient it drives an outward (positive) current flow through the partially open channel. Current can only flows when there is a non-zero conductance and a non-zero voltage gradient. This is called the ‘tail current’. \left\lbrack K^{+} \right\rbrack_{i} \left\lbrack K^{+} \right\rbrack_{o} \left\lbrack K^{+} \right\rbrack_{o} Note that the simulation above includes the Nernst equation with its dependence on the concentrations= 90mM and= 3mM. By raising the external potassium concentration to= 10mM you will then seethe Nernst potential increase from -85mV to -55mV and a negative (inward) current flowing during the period when the membrane voltage is clamped to -85mV. The cell is now in a ‘hyper-polarised’ state because the potential is less than the equilibrium potential.
