title: Solution methods for sets of Ordinary Differential Equations
content: ode-solutions Solution methods for sets of Ordinary Differential Equations toctree-wrapper Generator code Differential equations provide the governing mathematics for most of the simulations used in computational biology and engineering. This document is a very simplified explanation for how the output files from the libCellmlfunctionality can be coupled with a numerical integrator in order to solve generated model’s system of equations. The basic premise of differential equations is that we know something about a derivative quantity (like speed) which we need to integrate to find the variable we care about (like distance). Because most numerical integrators can handle only one order of differentiation, in situations involving higher orders (like acceleration) or more than one variable of influence or interest (like drag) we need to use a set of connected equations instead of one alone. Some examples: For each variable we need to know: \frac{dy}{dt}=f(y,t) A simple first order system,representing the effects of speed-dependent drag forces on the motion of an object; \frac{d^2y}{dt^2} = f(\frac {dy}{dt}, y, t) A higher-order system,representing a spring-mass-damper system like that in shock absorbers; and \frac{dy_1}{dt} = f(y_1, y_2, t) A multi-variable system,representing population dynamics between predator and prey species over time. - gradient function evaluation How to evaluate the variable’s first derivative as the solution progresses. This is known as thestep, and includes anything and everything - including other unknown variables, constants etc - which affect the gradient of this variable. An initial condition for the variable so we have somewhere to start from. If this value is dependent on other variable values, it must nonetheless be able to be determined at the time of submitting the solver. How large a step to take through the independent variable space (like time). This is often made consistent between all variables so that their solutions progress together, though adaptive step sizing methods (in which the step size adjusts itself during the solution process) and mixed methods (in which different steps are used for different variables) are possible. In the examples here we will use a constant step-size for all variables and all steps. - Modelling situation: Swimming with sharks Generator code The remainder of this document will use the following modelling situation to explain theoutputs. Imagine a tank filled with sharks and fishes; predators and prey. The population of fish can only grow when they are not being constantly eaten by sharks, and the rate at which is grows will depend on how many fish are available for breeding. At the same time, the population of sharks will depend on how much food is available in the fish population. In maths this relationship can be written: (a, b, c, d)=(1.2, -0.6, -0.8, 0.3) y_s(t=0)=2.0 y_f(t=0)=1.0 where the constantsand we’ll use the initial condtions ofand. \frac{dy_s}{dt} =f(sharks, fishes, time) = a y_s + b y_s y_f \frac{dy_f}{dt} =f(sharks, fishes, time) = c y_f + d y_s y_f ode_solutions True False True preserve Interpretation in code: MathML, generated C, generated Python Generator code Tutorial 3 False https://libcellml.org/documentation/latest/tutorials/tutorial3/index Theinterprets your CellML model into a structure which can easily be passed to a numerical integrator. Consider the file created by completingto model a coupled system of first-order ODEs. The governing equations - that is, gradient of each variable as a function of all the others - were specified in the MathML block within the CellML as: d d=a+b+c+0.5 For the sake of illustrating the different variable types, we’ll also define the (otherwise) constantusing the simple equation: interpretation-in-code-mathml-generated-c-generated-python interpretation\ in\ code:\ mathml,\ generated\ c,\ generated\ python modelling-situation-swimming-with-sharks modelling\ situation:\ swimming\ with\ sharks Classification of variables Generator code Variable code Component code Theclassifies all theitems within eachas: implementationCode code Generator code We can see this results of this classification process in the generated code returned by a call to thefunction of the. Variables : These do not require integration, but come in three types: CONSTANT code x = 3 variables do not need any kind of calculation, and is initialised directly using a constant value (for example,); COMPUTED_CONSTANT code x = y+5 variables only need calculation in order to set their initial (and thereafter constant) value (for example,); and ALGEBRAIC code x = 3yz y z variables are computed based on the values of other variables (for example,whereand/orare not constant). - Variables of integration <bvar> code <diff> code : Also called “base variables”, “free variables”, or “VOI”, these are specified by thechild of theoperator. VOI must not be initialised. States <ci> code <diff> code : These are those variables which need integration by a solver. They are theoperand of theoperator. - C++ panel-0-Qysr true sphinx-tabs-tab code-tab group-tab tab-0-Qysr Qysr tab 0 Python panel-0-UHl0aG9u false sphinx-tabs-tab code-tab group-tab tab-0-UHl0aG9u UHl0aG9u tab -1 Tabbed content closeable tablist tab-0-Qysr sphinx-tabs-panel code-tab group-tab panel-0-Qysr Qysr tabpanel 0 tab-0-UHl0aG9u sphinx-tabs-panel code-tab group-tab true panel-0-UHl0aG9u UHl0aG9u tabpanel 0 sphinx-tabs tab-element classification-of-variables classification\ of\ variables Defining the initial values Variables code setInitialValue code <bvar></bvar> code Generator code Allitems must either be initialised using thefunction, or specified within the MathML as a variable of integration (VOI) using thetags. Errors will be returned by thewhere variables are either: The initial conditions for all non-VOI variables are specified in a function, as opposed to the dictonary syntax used for the information items above. This function can be called by the solver whenever the solution’s initial conditions need to be reset. are not a VOI and are missing an initial value, or are a VOI and have been initalised. - defining-the-initial-values defining\ the\ initial\ values Specification of states and rates Variable code Generator code rates code state Once ahas been identified as avariable, it is paired by theby its corresponding entry in thearray, which represents its gradient function. states code computeRates code compute_rates code Because the gradients of each of the integrated variables orcould include dependency on time or any variable’s value, the values of each gradient function array must be updated throughout the solution process. This is done by calling the(in C) or(in Python) function to recalculate the gradient values for each state variable. solving-the-model Solving the model Euler’s method False https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Euler_method gradient function evaluation A simple numerical integration method likeinvolves stepping from the variable’s initial value, along the gradient (as evaluated by the), to a new value. This new value is used in turn to update all of the other gradient functions for other variables, and the process is repeated for all variables and all time points within the solution time interval. More complicated stepping procedures involve the combination of many points at which to evaluate the gradient function as well as relaxation and hybrid techniques. In this example we will use Euler’s method as being the simplest possible. Euler’s method updates each variable according to: f(t_k, x_k,...) gradient functions where the gradients of each variable are given by evaluating the current value of the appropriate gradient function,. It is the evaluation of thesewhich defines the nature of the physical situation represented by the model. Simple implementations are provided which will take the generated files, from both the C and Python generator profiles, and solve the system of equations. Solver tutorial False https://libcellml.org/documentation/solver Simple solver routines have been provided for you for both the C and Python profiles. Please see thefor details. title: A model of ion channel gating and current
content: ion-gates A model of ion channel gating and current HH Tutorial 5 False https://libcellml.org/documentation/latest/tutorials/hh_tutorial5/index This chapter describes a generic ion channel, and provides a foundation for later chapters where specific channels of sodium and potassium are described. The theory here is the basis for the practical modelling tutorial in. Chemical theory and entropy id15 chemical-theory-and-entropy Electrical theory id16 electrical-theory Mathematical modelling id17 mathematical-modelling Simulation and results id18 simulation-and-results Next steps id19 next-steps contents local contents contents Chemical theory and entropy id15 1 ion_gates id1 id7 A good example of a model based on a first order equation is the one used by Hodgkin and Huxleyto describe the gating behaviour of an ion channel. Before we describe the gating behaviour of an ion channel, however, we need to explain the concepts of the ‘Nernst potential’ and channel conductance. Y^{+} Y^{-} \left\lbrack Y^{+} \right\rbrack_{o} \left\lbrack Y^{+} \right\rbrack_{i} entropy An ion channel is a protein or protein complex embedded in the bi-lipid membrane surrounding a cell and containing a pore through which an ion(or) can pass when the channel is open. If the concentration of this ion isoutside the cell andinside the cell, the force driving an ion through the pore is calculated from the change in. Gate status for a single gate, and current through four gates {'*': 'images/tut5.png'} images/tut5.png \gamma=4 Dynamics of opening status for a single gate, and the resulting current forgates in series. center ion-gate-status ion_gate_status simulation-and-results simulation\ and\ results Next steps id19 \alpha_{y} \beta_{y} y < 1 \left( y_{\infty} \right)^{\gamma}{.\overset{\overline{}}{g}}_{Y} y_{\infty} = \frac{\alpha_{y}}{\alpha_{y} + \beta_{y}} \tau = \frac{1}{\alpha_{y} + \beta_{y}} potassium std std-ref True potassium_channel#potassium-channel sodium ion channels std std-ref True sodium_channel#sodium-channel The model of a gated ion channel presented here is used in the next two sections for the neural potassium and sodium channels. The gates create the transience of the channel’s conductance through the voltage dependence of the gating rate constantsand. This means that the channel conductance (including the open channel conductance) is voltage dependent. For a partially open channel (), the steady state conductance is, where. The gating time constantsare therefore also voltage dependent. Both of these voltage dependent factors of ion channel gating are important in explaining channel properties, as is described in the next sections for the neuraland. title: Modelling theory and background

title: The Hodgkin-Huxley model of nerve action potential
content: hodgkin-huxley-model The Hodgkin-Huxley model of nerve action potential 1 hodgkin_huxley_model id1 id4 “their discoveries concerning the ionic mechanisms involved in excitation and inhibition in the peripheral and central portions of the nerve cell membrane” Here we describe the first (and most famous) model of nerve fibre electrophysiology based on the membrane ion channels that we have discussed in the last two sections. This is the work by Alan Hodgkin and Andrew Huxley in 1952that won them (together with John Eccles) the 1963 Noble prize in Physiology or Medicine for. Theory: The cable equation id7 theory-the-cable-equation Theory: Action potentials id8 theory-action-potentials Interpretation as a CellML model id9 interpretation-as-a-cellml-model Simulation and results id10 simulation-and-results contents local contents contents Theory: The cable equation id7 cable equation 1 hodgkin_huxley_model id2 id5 Thewas developed in 1890to predict the degradation of an electrical signal passing along the transatlantic cable. It is derived as follows: Figure 20 std std-numref True leaky-cable-current-flow Figure %s i_a A If the voltage is raised at the left hand end of the cable (shown by the deep red in), an axial current(in) will flow. This current depends on the voltage gradient according to Ohm’s Law, such that: \frac{\partial V}{\partial x} V/m r_a \Omega/m whereis the voltage gradient along the cable (in), andis the resistance (in). i_m A/m If the cable membrane leaks a current of() per unit length, then conservation of current gives: i_a and, substituting forwe get: i_m C_m \mu F/cm^2 C_m\frac{\partial V}{\partial t} i_{leak} There are two sources of membrane leakage current,. First, the capacitance associated with the membrane,(approximately 1) results in a current of, and secondly the holes or channels in the membrane give. Incorporating these into the equation above gives: cable equation r_a Rearranging gives the(for constant): current density \mu A/cm^2 where all terms represent(current per membrane area) and have units of. Is one side in/out the cell? center neuron-current-flow neuron_current_flow 600px Current-voltage trajectory {'*': 'images/current_voltage_trajectory.png'} images/current_voltage_trajectory.png Current-voltage trajectory during an action potential. center current-voltage-during-action-potential current_voltage_during_action_potential CellML schematic cell membrane model {'*': 'images/hodgkin_1952.png'} images/hodgkin_1952.png + + Na K L A schematic cell diagram describing the current flows across the cell bi-lipid membrane that are captured in the Hodgkin-Huxley model. The membrane ion channels are a sodium (Na) channel, a potassium (K) channel, and a leakage (L) channel (for chloride and other ions) through which the currents I, Iand Iflow, respectively. center hodgkin-huxley-schematic hodgkin_huxley_schematic 500px inward outward i_{Na} i_K i_L = g_L\left( V - E_L \right) Figure 21 std std-numref True neuron-current-flow Figure %s TODO The cable equation above can be used to model the propagation of an action potential along a neuron or any other excitable cell. The leak current is associated primarily with the inward movement of sodium ions through the membrane’s sodium channel, giving themembrane current, and the outward movement of potassium ions through a membrane potassium channel, giving thecurrent(see). A further small leak currentassociated with chloride and other ions is also included.Need to define E_L and g_L ?? How is i_L related to i_leak above?? V Na^+ K^+ Figure 22 std std-numref True current-voltage-during-action-potential Figure %s When the membrane potentialrises due to axial current flow, the sodium () channels open and the potassium () channels close, such that the membrane potential moves towards the Nernst potential for sodium. The subsequent decline of the Na channel conductance and the increasing K channel conductance as the voltage drops rapidly repolarises the membrane to its resting potential of -85mV (see). 1 hodgkin_huxley_model id3 id6 If we assume that the whole cell is clamped with an axially-uniform potential then we can neglectthe term in the equation above representing the rate of change of axial current along the cable: V and so obtain the membrane potentialby integrating the first order ODE: TODO How are the i_L and I_L in the figure related? Consistent labelling needed? - \frac{1}{r_a}\frac{\partial^{2}V}{\partial x^2} \approx 0 hodgkin_huxley_model True False True preserve \frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{- \left( i_{Na} + \ i_K + i_L \right)}{C_m} hodgkin_huxley_model True False True preserve theory-action-potentials theory:\ action\ potentials Interpretation as a CellML model id9 encapsulation previous section on the sodium channel std std-ref True sodium_channel#sodium-channel Figure 24 std std-numref True cellml-of-hh-model Figure %s We discussed the idea and implementation ofin the, and here it is no different. We would like to create a model with the encapsulation structure shown in. imports HH Tutorial 2 Creating a model which uses imports HH Tutorial 2 Creating a model which uses imports https://libcellml.org/documentation/latest/tutorials/hh_tutorial2/index As with other aspects of libCellML, there are several options for the model construction process. Since we already have potassium and sodium channel models available, it would make sense to be able to reuse these here. This functionality requires; their use is demonstrated in. HH Tutorial 1 Creating a model using the API HH Tutorial 1 Creating a model using the API https://libcellml.org/documentation/latest/tutorials/hh_tutorial1/index HH Tutorial 3 Debugging a model HH Tutorial 3 Debugging a model https://libcellml.org/documentation/latest/tutorials/hh_tutorial3/index Importing allows all or part of a model to be used in-situ, without needing to manually parse its CellML file and instantiate it as an additional model (as has been the procedure inand). In this instance, the models described earlier representing aand a, as well as one to represent the leakage concept introduced above can be combined to make the full Hodgkin-Huxley model.
